combinations.workload: A coding agent that runs a large monorepo review: the agent loads a ~700K-token snapshot of the repo (architecture docs, key modules, dependency graph) once, then iterates across hundreds of tool calls (grep, file reads, lint runs) over a long session reviewing a 40-file PR. Programmatic tool calling orchestrates the grep/read/test loop in code so the raw tool outputs (megabytes of file contents and test logs) never re-enter the model context.
combinations.compounded_value: The compound is a cost cliff, not a discount. The 1M window makes the huge repo snapshot loadable at all; prompt caching makes re-reading that 700K prefix on every one of the hundreds of turns cost ~10% instead of full-rate; programmatic tool calling keeps the fan-out of noisy tool outputs (which would otherwise bloat the cached-and-billed context and force cache invalidation) entirely out of the billed window. Cache + 1M alone still pays full rate on tool output growth; PTC alone has no giant stable prefix to amortize. Together you get a near-flat per-turn cost on a session that would otherwise grow superlinearly.
combinations.competitor_best_stack: OpenAI can stack automatic prompt caching + a 1M-context model (GPT-4.1) + function calling, but function results return into context (no allowed_callers equivalent that keeps outputs out of the model's window), so tool-output growth re-bills and can evict the cached prefix. Gemini has explicit/implicit context caching + 1M/2M window + function calling but likewise no out-of-context tool-output orchestration. Both match two of three legs; neither matches the PTC leg, so the compounded cost flatness is a genuine Claude-only edge.
combinations.skeptic_why: OpenAI's GPT-4.1 1M + automatic caching + Code Interpreter/function-calling can keep raw outputs out of context but either re-bills the cached prefix per tool round-trip or can't run real monorepo lint/test infra in-sandbox, so it can't reproduce PTC's in-code external-tool fan-out at flat cost.

combinations.workload: A long-running customer-support / ops triage agent that runs continuous multi-hour sessions over a queue of hundreds of tickets, accumulating tool-call transcripts (API lookups, log pulls) that blow past the window. Context editing clears stale tool_uses mid-session; the memory tool persists the durable facts (account state, prior resolution, escalation notes) to a file store before the clear.
combinations.compounded_value: Compound on cost AND reliability. Context editing's clear_tool_uses normally torpedoes the cache (clearing the prefix invalidates the cached segment); memory tool lets you evacuate the must-keep facts to disk first, so after the clear you re-prime a SMALL stable prefix that re-caches cheaply, while the cleared bulk never re-bills. Memory alone doesn't reduce in-context billing; context-editing alone destroys cache value and risks dropping facts the agent still needs. Stacked, you get unbounded session length at bounded per-turn cost with no fact loss.
combinations.competitor_best_stack: OpenAI's Responses API has server-side state + automatic caching, but no first-class clear-tool-results primitive that preserves a re-cacheable prefix, and memory is via the (separate) Assistants/file-search store rather than a model-driven memory tool. Gemini has context caching but no model-initiated context-editing clear. Neither can reproduce the 'evacuate-then-clear-then-recache' loop as a coordinated stack; closest is OpenAI but it leaks cleared content back into billed state.
combinations.conflict: context editing clear_tool_uses interacts badly with advisor blocks — safe here because no advisor tool is in this stack, but do NOT add advisor to this combination.
combinations.skeptic_why: OpenAI Responses API with client-side context pruning of stale tool_uses + your own fact store + automatic prefix caching reproduces the evacuate-then-clear-then-recache loop, since you fully control the message array and the cache is keyed on the surviving stable prefix exactly like Claude's.

combinations.workload: Overnight ingestion of a 50,000-document contract/financial-filing corpus where every document is run against the SAME large extraction rubric + few-shot exemplar set (~30K-token instruction prefix). Each batch request = the shared cached rubric prefix + one native-PDF document, extracting structured fields.
combinations.compounded_value: Two multiplicative discounts on the same dollar: Batch API gives 50% off, and prompt caching gives ~90% off the shared 30K rubric prefix that repeats across all 50K requests. Native PDF input removes a whole OCR/preprocessing pipeline (and its cost/error surface) by letting the model read the PDF (text + layout + embedded images) directly. Batch alone discounts everything once; caching alone discounts the prefix at full per-request rate; together the dominant cost term (rubric × 50K) is cut by both factors and the document-handling cost goes to zero.
combinations.competitor_best_stack: OpenAI Batch API (50% off) + automatic prompt caching + direct PDF in the Responses API is a near-complete match — this one is close. The remaining Claude edge is thinner: cache-hit economics and PDF fidelity (Claude reads layout/visual PDF structure natively in one call), but a founder could plausibly get parity on cost from OpenAI. Be honest: competitor largely matches this stack; the edge is marginal, mostly accuracy-of-extraction on layout-heavy PDFs rather than a clean cost cliff.
combinations.skeptic_why: OpenAI Batch (50%) + automatic prompt caching + direct PDF in the Responses API hits the same two multiplicative discounts on the same rubric×50K dominant term, and the residual is layout-extraction accuracy, not a cost cliff (the prompt itself concedes parity).

combinations.workload: A high-volume routing layer in front of a product agent: every inbound request first hits a cheap Haiku call running the advisor tool (out-of-band guidance/policy reasoning) to decide difficulty and policy path, which then routes easy traffic to low-effort Haiku/Sonnet and hard/risky traffic to high-effort Opus. Think 1M requests/day support or moderation triage.
combinations.compounded_value: Compound on cost-per-resolved-request. The effort knob lets the SAME model spend less compute on easy cases; tier selection sends most volume to Haiku; the advisor tool makes the cheap router smart enough to mis-route rarely (so few requests get wastefully escalated AND few hard ones slip through under-resourced). Each alone is a blunt instrument: effort knob without good routing still runs everything on one tier; tiers without an advisor router mis-classify; advisor without the knob/tiers can't act on its own judgment. Stacked, the blended cost per request drops while keeping hard-case accuracy.
combinations.competitor_best_stack: OpenAI has reasoning_effort (on o-series/GPT-5 class) + a tier ladder (nano/mini/full) + function-calling-based routing, and Gemini has thinking budgets + Flash/Pro tiers — both can build a difficulty router. The advisor tool's out-of-context guidance shape is the only non-replicable leg, and a competitor can approximate it with a separate router call. Competitors can assemble a close-equivalent stack; the Claude edge is modest and rests on advisor's cleaner separation, not a hard moat.
combinations.conflict: advisor blocks are not fully compatible with context editing clear_tool_uses — keep context editing out of this stack.
combinations.skeptic_why: OpenAI reasoning_effort + nano/mini/full tier ladder + a cheap dedicated router call (or Gemini thinking-budgets + Flash/Pro + router) builds the same cost-per-resolved-request funnel, and a separate router call approximates the advisor tool's out-of-band guidance well enough.

combinations.workload: A financial-analytics agent that turns natural-language questions into validated computations over uploaded datasets at scale: a large cached system prefix (schema + business rules + formula library), structured output forces the model to emit a typed computation plan, and code execution actually runs the numbers in a sandbox rather than letting the model hallucinate arithmetic. Hundreds of analyst queries against the same dataset/schema.
combinations.compounded_value: Compound on accuracy with a cost kicker. Structured outputs guarantee the plan is machine-parseable (no parse failures → no silent wrong path); code execution guarantees the arithmetic/aggregation is actually correct (eliminates the model's numeric hallucination, the dominant error class in analytics agents); prompt caching makes the heavy schema/formula prefix nearly free across hundreds of queries so you can afford to include the full grounding context every time. Structured-only still hallucinates numbers; code-exec-only can emit malformed plans that crash the runner. Together: typed plan + verified math + cheap full context = a measurably lower wrong-answer rate per dollar.
combinations.competitor_best_stack: OpenAI matches this most closely: Structured Outputs (strict JSON schema) + Code Interpreter + prompt caching is a real, assemblable stack. Gemini has controlled generation + code execution + context caching. This is largely matched — be honest. The residual Claude edge is reliability of the structured+tool interaction under load, not a unique capability. Treat this as parity-leaning.
combinations.conflict: none — but note Citations and tool-returned search results both 400 with structured outputs, so this stack must NOT add either; it relies on code-execution grounding instead of citation grounding.
combinations.skeptic_why: OpenAI Structured Outputs (strict schema) + Code Interpreter + automatic caching is an assemblable typed-plan + verified-math + cheap-grounding stack that matches the wrong-answer-rate-per-dollar target, as the prompt itself admits parity.

combinations.workload: A devops/incident-response agent: when paged, it pulls live telemetry and runbooks through MCP connectors (Grafana, PagerDuty, internal tools), loads a library of org-specific Agent Skills (deploy-rollback, db-failover, cache-flush procedures as reusable skill modules), and uses extended thinking to plan a multi-step remediation across the connected systems. Bursty, low-volume-but-high-stakes incidents.
combinations.compounded_value: Compound on reliability (mean-time-to-recovery and correctness of irreversible actions). MCP gives live, real-world state instead of stale assumptions; Agent Skills package the org's vetted procedures so the agent doesn't improvise dangerous steps; extended thinking lets it sequence the remediation and check preconditions before acting. MCP alone gives data but no procedure; Skills alone are static playbooks with no live state; thinking alone reasons over nothing real. Stacked: grounded state + vetted procedure + deliberate planning = far fewer wrong irreversible actions during incidents.
combinations.competitor_best_stack: OpenAI now supports MCP (Responses API connectors) + extended reasoning, and can emulate 'skills' via tool/function definitions or a prompt library; Gemini supports MCP-style tool connection + thinking. Agent Skills as a first-class packaged-procedure primitive is the Claude-distinct leg, but competitors can approximate it with structured tool/prompt bundles. Competitor stack is close; the edge is operational ergonomics and reuse, not a hard capability gap.
combinations.skeptic_why: OpenAI Responses MCP connectors + extended reasoning + a versioned tool/prompt bundle emulating Skills delivers grounded-state + vetted-procedure + deliberate-planning, and the only gap is packaging ergonomics, not an MTTR-affecting capability.

combinations.workload: A back-office automation founder running thousands of independent long-horizon tasks overnight (e.g., reconcile 5,000 vendor invoices, each requiring a multi-step agent: fetch → match → flag exceptions → draft email). Each task is a Managed Agent instance executing a shared invoice-reconciliation Skill, dispatched in bulk via the Batch API.
combinations.compounded_value: Compound on cost-at-scale for agentic (not single-shot) workloads. Batch API normally discounts single completions; pairing it with Managed Agents lets you get the 50% batch economics on entire multi-step agent runs, not just one call — the discount now covers the whole orchestration. Agent Skills make each agent instance consistent and cheap to spin up (no re-teaching the procedure per task). Batch alone can't run a multi-turn agent loop; Managed Agents alone run at full real-time rate; Skills alone don't change pricing. Stacked: discounted, consistent, hands-off overnight agentic throughput.
combinations.competitor_best_stack: OpenAI and Gemini do not need to match batch-priced managed agent loops because Claude Managed Agents sessions are not batch-discountable either. The current Claude pricing docs state that the Batch API discount does not apply to Managed Agents sessions because sessions are stateful and interactive and there is no batch mode. The proposed cost compound therefore collapses on Claude before the competitor comparison.
combinations.conflict: Batch API discount does not apply to Managed Agents sessions; current Claude pricing says sessions are stateful and interactive and there is no batch mode.
combinations.skeptic_why: Claude Managed Agents sessions are not batch-eligible: the pricing docs say the Batch API discount does not apply to Managed Agents sessions because sessions are stateful and interactive and there is no batch mode.

note: Candidates only. A combination ships only after a demonstrator measures it at promotable_edge: true, the way engine/demonstrators/grounding_stack.py proves its stack. competitor_best_stack and the skeptic verdict are the internal both-directions read.
